title: Princeton Summer Journalism Program (PSJP)
description:
The Princeton Summer Journalism Program (PSJP) is one of the only programs of its kind offering a free, innovative residential journalism and college prep institute for high achieving high school juniors from low-income backgrounds.
The Princeton Summer Journalism Program is a year-long college preparation program for high school juniors who are interested in journalism. For the summer, the program will take advantage of lessons learned during our three virtual summers to offer our first ever multi-week, hybrid summer intensive, which will culminate in our annual 10-day residential institute on the Princeton University campus in New Jersey.
Participants will attend online workshops and lectures with renowned journalists from all over the country throughout the month of July. During this time, students complete weekly reading assignments to stay abreast of current events, begin preparing their own articles, and work with their assigned college counselor to complete components of their college applications. During our residential institute, students will visit Princeton from their hometown or state and stay in University undergraduate dorms, attending workshops and lectures and learning skills to produce their own newspaper. Potential activities can include touring major news organizations, such as The New York Times, Huffington Post and Bloomberg; covering a professional sports event; cover news events in the Princeton area; film and producing a short documentary; attending a film or theatre production; conducting an investigative project; authoring a group blog; and reporting, writing, editing, and designing their own newspaper, The Princeton Summer Journal, which will be published on the program's last day.
In addition to introducing students to the multifaceted world of journalism, PSJP is designed to immerse students in a residential experience at one of the best colleges in the country. Participants live in dorms, eat in dining halls, tour campus, and attend lectures led by Princeton professors and campus-life staff. During the last week of the program, students attend seminars on every aspect of the college admissions process, including writing personal narratives, choosing the best fit college, and understanding how applications are evaluated, and meet with colleges admissions representatives.

trpcData.admissionInfo: Students apply in the winter-spring of their junior year and participate in the program during the summer following their junior year through the end of their senior year. No exceptions will be made for students in any other grades
trpcData.eligibilityInfo:
Requirements:
- Must be a current high school junior at the time of application. No exceptions will be made for students in other grades.
- Must reside in the United States and intend to attend college in the U.S. Students living or attending school abroad, including U.S. citizens, are not eligible.
- Must have a minimum unweighted GPA of 3.5 on a 4.0 scale.
- Must meet at least one of the following socioeconomic conditions:
- The combined income of parent(s)/guardian(s), including child support payments, does not exceed $60,000.
- Eligible for free or reduced-price lunch in high school.
- Eligible for an SAT or ACT fee waiver.
- Must have an interest in journalism.

Preferences:
- First-generation college students receive priority consideration.
- Applications are evaluated holistically, and students with extenuating personal or academic circumstances may be considered on a case-by-case basis.

title: iGEM Competition
description:
The iGEM Competition, organized by the iGEM Foundation, is a global annual event in synthetic biology, targeting undergraduate, high school, and graduate students. The competition focuses on enabling multidisciplinary teams to tackle real-world challenges through synthetic biology. Participants design, build, test, and measure a system using interchangeable biological parts and standard molecular biology techniques. The competition encourages teams to create innovative projects that positively impact their communities and the world, culminating in the Grand Jamboree, where projects are presented and judged.

Key topics and skills covered in the iGEM Competition include synthetic biology, molecular biology techniques, and project design and management. Teams work both inside and outside the lab, engaging in hands-on experimentation and documentation. The competition promotes collaboration across various disciplines, including biology, engineering, computer science, art, and more, emphasizing a diverse team composition.

The iGEM Competition involves both online and in-person components. Teams interact and collaborate online, while the Grand Jamboree provides an opportunity for in-person presentations and networking. The competition spans several months, with participants dedicating significant time to developing their projects. Important deadlines include registration and submission requirements, with the Grand Jamboree typically held in November.

Each team must have a Principal Investigator (PI) who provides guidance and manages official responsibilities such as registration and safety compliance. Teams are also required to have an academic instructor and are encouraged to seek additional advisors and mentors. Student team members must meet age requirements and complete registration steps. Participants benefit from gaining practical experience in synthetic biology, project management, and interdisciplinary collaboration, preparing them for future scientific and professional endeavors.

- Regular and late registration details are available on the How to Register page.
trpcData.eligibilityInfo:
Requirements:
- Must be part of a team composed primarily of undergraduate students, but teams may also include postgraduate students, high school students, or community lab members.
- Student team members must have an iGEM user account, enter their age information, and join the online team roster by the Team Roster Freeze.
- Teams must include everyone involved, such as professors, instructors, advisors, and student members, on the Team Roster by the roster freeze to be officially recognized as participants.
- For collegiate teams, the team's section is determined automatically based on the ages of the student team members.
- Commercial teams, run by companies, must register as commercial teams and are not eligible to compete if registered as other types.

Preferences:
- Teams are encouraged to correctly register as the appropriate team kind to ensure participation and credit for their work.

title: Intro to Video Game Design
description:
"Intro to Video Game Design" is an enriching course offered by the University of Rochester that imparts essential knowledge and skills for aspiring game designers. The main objective of this course is to help students understand the art of storytelling in video game design and the techniques used to create puzzles, action-adventure, and drama. Participants will learn about game design theories, the evolution of video games from Atari to Xbox and mobile gaming, and gain the ability to customize their own templated video game.

The course is delivered entirely online, offering flexibility and allowing students to learn at their own pace. It requires 20-25 hours of total instruction and coursework which includes engaging multimedia, simulations, and curated assignments with guidance and support. Students can expect to work with GameMaker Studio 2 to customize a pre-built demo and turn it into a 2D platforming game of their own design.

The duration of the program spans multiple 2- and 4-week sessions. Regarding the instructors, the course is taught by Dr. Joseph A. Loporcaro, an Assistant Professor at the University of Rochester. He teaches both Digital Media Studies and Computer Science and has a rich background in all areas of mass media, with a specific focus on video games. In addition, course mentors like Tara, an Economics undergraduate, and Emersyn, a Computer Science major, will provide guidance, feedback, and support throughout the course.

To be eligible for the course, students must be at least 13 years old. Though the program is designed for enrichment and does not offer college credit, students will receive an official Certificate of Completion from the University of Rochester if they complete at least 70% of the course assignments and the final project. This certificate can be included in resumes or college applications, adding value to their higher education experiences. The main outcome of this program is a comprehensive understanding of video game design, equipping students with the skills and knowledge to determine if this is a field they’d like to pursue further.

title: Orthopedic Medicine: Treatments & Surgery
description:
The "Orthopedic Medicine: Treatments & Surgery" course from the University of Rochester is an opportunity for high school students who are interested in medicine, surgery, or orthopedics to delve into one of the top medical specialties. The course provides a comprehensive understanding of common orthopedic conditions and how they present in the body. Participants will study a range of issues including tumors, trauma, total joint replacement, and pediatric issues related to growth and development. They will also explore the treatment of various conditions such as arthritis, osteoporosis, degenerative joint disease, and others.

The course is delivered online and covers several subspecialties in the field of orthopedics. The curriculum includes a thorough introduction to orthopedics, identification of the areas treated by an orthopedic surgeon, a review of non-operative tools, and operations using implants. It also offers participants a unique perspective on a patient's journey through the diagnosis and treatment of bone cancer, differentiating between benign and malignant tumors, and exploring joint replacement, amputation, and artificial limbs.

The University of Rochester offers this course in multiple 2- and 4-week sessions, making it a flexible opportunity for students aged 13 and above. The fee for the course is $1495. However, it's essential to note that the course is competitive, and early application is encouraged.

Upon completion of the course, students will be able to name common orthopedic conditions, understand the roles of surgical and non-surgical interventions, identify the different occupations of an orthopedic team, and determine if orthopedic medicine is a field they'd like to pursue. Additionally, the course helps to develop communication and presentation skills, which are essential for a future career in medicine.

title: Personalized Medicine: Customizing Care Through Genetics
description:
"Personalized Medicine: Customizing Care Through Genetics" is an online course provided by the University of Rochester which aims to give high school students an understanding of human genetics and its application in personalized healthcare. The course seeks to answer questions regarding individual responses to therapies and genetic predispositions to diseases, allowing students to explore the intricacies of genomic sequencing, gene data reading, and mutation identification that leads to diseases. It also provides insights into how genomic information contributes to uniqueness in individuals, along with the technological advancements in personalized medicine that are shaping the future of healthcare.

The course covers key topics like the basics of genetic code and the variation of simple gene sequences. It extends to understanding how these genetic variations create a diverse range of human traits, from physical characteristics like hair color to susceptibility to certain diseases. The course also elucidates how some traits are inherited and what determines the likelihood of inheriting a particular disease.

Offered in multiple 2- and 4-week sessions, the course is open to students aged 13 and above. The sessions, regardless of their duration, provide identical educational content, learning materials, and number of assignments, making them adaptable to the students' schedules. The course culminates in a Capstone project that allows students to demonstrate their understanding and receive feedback on their work.

While instructor details are not specified, students can expect to learn from experienced mentors in the field. The course, however, does not mention any specific prerequisites or eligibility criteria other than the age limit. Upon completion, students can expect to have a foundational understanding of genetics and its application in personalized medicine, equipping them for future studies in pre-med or STEM fields.

title: StandOut Connect
description:
StandOut Connect is a program that offers participants access to online educational resources, assignments, and virtual meetings. The main objective of StandOut Connect is to facilitate connections between students and opportunities for mentoring or employment. This is achieved by guiding students in creating effective resumes following the guidelines provided in the StandOut Connect syllabus. Notably, these resumes are then shared with up to 6 opportunities for potential meetings. 

The format of the StandOut Connect program is primarily online, encompassing virtual meetings and assignments. If a participant is unable to attend a meeting, they can access recordings of the meetings. It's important to note that the program operates on a paid tuition basis; the full tuition amount must be remitted as outlined in the admissions email in order to participate.

Application Timeline:
- For the Fall program, the Early Application Deadline is typically in early July, and the Regular Application Deadline is typically in early August. All students will receive their admissions decisions via email by September at the latest.
- For the Winter program, the Early Application Deadline is typically in mid-October, and the Regular Application Deadline is typically at the end of November. All students will receive their admissions decisions via email by late December at the latest.

How to Apply:
- Applicants can apply directly via the program's website.

Application Costs:
- The program has a tuition fee of $1,950. This cost covers the program instruction, company sourcing & vetting, and a scholarship program for low-income students. Those in need of financial assistance should indicate this need within their application.

Acceptance Rate/Competitiveness:
- The acceptance rate varies from cohort to cohort, and is generally a little higher during the school year than during the summer. The average acceptance rate for 2023 across all programs was 11.4%.

Other:
- Applicants who need financial assistance to participate should indicate this in their application. The program offers scholarships to as many students as possible.
- If an applicant does not receive an email about their application, this means they were not granted admission. However, the program encourages such applicants to apply again in the future.
trpcData.eligibilityInfo:
Requirements:
- Any student who is 15+ is eligible
- International students must demonstrate English fluency and be willing to work on a US time zone

title: Physics Bowl
description:
The PhysicsBowl is an international high school competition that challenges students in physics through a timed, multiple-choice exam. The competition is divided into two divisions: Division I for first-year physics students and Division II for second-year students. Participants compete regionally, with specialized math and science schools, test prep centers, and educational services assigned to specific regions to ensure a fair comparison of scores. 

The contest takes place between March 19 and April 4, and covers topics typically found in high school physics curricula. Teams are scored based on the top five individual scores from each school, contributing to the overall team ranking. Students are recognized for their performance, and high achievers earn international recognition for both themselves and their schools.

The PhysicsBowl encourages participation from schools worldwide, with seven dedicated regions for countries in Asia and the Middle East. Proctoring is a critical component of the competition, and strict guidelines are in place to maintain the integrity of the exam. Proctors must be independent and have a minimum of a two-year degree, though not necessarily in physics.

title: PitchIllinois
description:
PitchIllinois is a free hackathon designed by high school students for their peers across Illinois and the Midwest. The event spans 12 hours and aims to provide participants with a startup-like experience where they can collaborate on building projects from scratch, attend hands-on workshops, and pitch their ideas to a panel of judges for feedback. The primary objective is to bridge the gap between learning computer science and applying it to make a tangible difference
.
The hackathon covers a variety of key topics and skills, including project development, idea visualization, and pitching techniques. Participants are encouraged to create tech-related projects that can help the local Champaign-Urbana community, with support and feedback from judges to further develop their ideas.
PitchIllinois will take place in person in late February from 8:30 am to 6 pm at the University Laboratory High School in Champaign, Illinois. Teams, typically consisting of 3-4 members, will be formed during the opening hours of the event, and participants do not need to come with a pre-formed group. The event is designed to be inclusive and welcoming to all skill levels, including complete beginners.

The hackathon is led by a team of student founders, including Bruce Tang (Founder, Executive Director), Noor Elbanna (Co-Founder, Design Team Lead), Felix Lee (Co-Founder, Systems Team Lead), and Henry Wang (Co-Founder, Experience Team Lead). The outcomes and benefits for participants include gaining practical experience in project development, learning new skills through workshops, and receiving valuable feedback from industry professionals.

title: Pomona College Academy for Youth Success
description:
The Pomona College Academy for Youth Success (PAYS) enrolls over 90 local high school students and seeks to support them in preparing for admission to selective and highly selective colleges and universities. PAYS scholars engage in a thought-provoking combination of courses, cultural events, workshops, projects and field trips during the intensive 4-week, residential program as well as throughout the academic year. PAYS serves rising sophomores through rising seniors from groups traditionally underrepresented in higher education—students who are first in their family to attend college; from low income families; and from African American, Latino, Native American, Pacific Islander, and/or part of other underrepresented racial or ethnic groups.
There is no cost for participation in PAYS. Pomona College covers all costs including housing, food, activities, and transportation to field trips. Students will only be required to bring basic school supplies, such as pencils and paper.

externalReviews.title: Answer to how prestigious?
externalReviews.content: It’s extremely prestigious, but if name-brand recognition is hugely important to you, then I would not recommend Pomona. Most people in academia think very highly of it, but more often than not, the average person may confuse it for Cal Poly Pomona or even the literal country of Panama...That being said, if you care about career and grad school opportunities, I would argue that Pomona is “top of the top.” Students get interviews at all leading investment banking and consulting firms, we have the 2nd most Fulbright Scholars per capita in the country, and we have one of the top med school acceptance rates in the country.

externalReviews.title: US News article on Pomona College
externalReviews.content: Pomona College is a private institution that was founded in 1887. It has a total undergraduate enrollment of 1,764 (fall 2021), its setting is suburban, and the campus size is 140 acres. It utilizes a semester-based academic calendar. Pomona College's ranking in the 2022-2023 edition of Best Colleges is National Liberal Arts Colleges, #3. Its tuition and fees are $59,238.

externalReviews.title: Niche Article on Pomona College
externalReviews.content: Pomona is also one of the 5 Claremont Colleges, a consortium enabling its students to cross-register for 2,700 classes and mingle with over 6,000 peers across our adjacent campuses. So even though our small classes (averaging 15), strength across the curriculum (with 48 majors) and tight-knit residential life (4 years guaranteed housing) might seem common, Pomona College is unique.

externalReviews.title: Forbes article
externalReviews.content: Pomona College in Claremont, CA. is one of the top liberal arts universities in the nation and the oldest member of the Claremont Colleges consortium. Pomona prides itself on small classes—often just 10 to 15 students—and close-knit relationships with faculty...The college offers 49 majors...The college is committed to need-blind admissions for U.S. citizens and seeks to enroll students from economically disadvantaged backgrounds. Pomona counts numerous distinguished alumni across many disciplines, including former Disney executive Roy E. Disney, Nobel Prize-winning biochemist Jennifer Doudna and civil rights activist Myrlie Evers-Williams.

title: Pre-College at CCA
description:
The Pre-College program at California College of the Arts is a four-week on-campus experience designed for high school students who have completed their sophomore, junior, or senior year. It offers an immersive environment where students can experience life at an art and design school, earn three college credits, and develop work suitable for college admissions portfolios. This program is accessible to a diverse group of students, including international attendees who meet English language requirements and have the opportunity to live in CCA residence halls.

The curriculum covers various art and design disciplines, allowing students to refine or develop new skills regardless of their previous experience level. Expert instructors guide students in foundational and advanced techniques to enhance their creative abilities. Participants leave the program not only with college credits but also with portfolio-ready pieces that can bolster their college applications.

title: Global Youth Entrepreneurship Challenge (GYEC)
description:
The Global Youth Entrepreneurship Challenge (GYEC) aims to inspire high school students aged 14 to 19 by fostering their entrepreneurial spirit and enhancing their understanding of business. It is a worldwide business idea competition where participants engage in a 12-hour challenge to address significant global issues such as poverty, hunger, education, energy, and climate change. The competition encourages the application of science and technology to create innovative and sustainable business ideas.

Participants are required to submit a two-page business plan and a three-minute video presentation within the 12-hour timeframe. The competition is conducted online, and the challenge is announced at 8:00 am, with submissions due by 20:00. The competition's format allows students to develop skills in time and project management, teamwork, creativity, and innovation. It also provides opportunities to present solutions professionally in both written and video formats.

All participants receive a Certificate of Participation, with winners receiving a trophy and an award certificate. The challenge is open to high school students who are enrolled in secondary education and aged between 14 and 19 years. Teams must consist of 3 to 8 eligible students. The GYEC offers a valuable experience for students to gain insights into international communities and develop skills necessary for business and entrepreneurship.

title: Maimonides Health Scholars
description:
The Maimonides Health Scholars program, offered by Maimonides Emergency Medicine, provides high school juniors and seniors, as well as college students, with an immersive experience in the Emergency Department (ED) of Maimonides Medical Center, a large teaching hospital in Brooklyn, New York. This unpaid internship aims to introduce students to the healthcare field and the principles of clinical medicine, making it ideal for those considering careers as physicians, nurses, nurse practitioners, physician assistants, physical therapists, psychologists, social workers, or paramedics.
 
 Participants in the program will engage in various activities, including assisting in patient care, improving communication with patients, and supporting ED staff. They will rotate through different areas of the ED and gain firsthand exposure to the operations of a busy urban emergency department. Interns will also have the opportunity to participate in ongoing ED research projects, provided they complete an additional training module.
 
 The program is delivered in-person, with several rotation periods throughout the year: April 1st to June 30th, June 16th to September 1st (Summer), September 2nd to January 4th (Fall), and January 5th to April 5th (Winter). Applications are reviewed on a rolling basis. Eligibility requires submission of application forms, a brief essay or video, documentation of vaccination status, a letter of recommendation, and participation in a 15-minute virtual interview. Accepted applicants will undergo further processing as volunteers, which includes an in-person interview and a mandatory 3-hour orientation. Through this comprehensive program, participants will gain valuable insights and experience that are foundational for a career in healthcare.

title: Spanish Language & Argentinian Culture
description:
The Spanish Language & Argentinian Culture program by CIEE offers a unique opportunity for high school students to enhance their Spanish skills while experiencing the vibrant culture of Buenos Aires, Argentina. This immersive program spans four weeks and is delivered in-person, allowing participants to live with host families and engage in authentic cultural experiences. The program is structured to accelerate Spanish language proficiency through daily language classes and interactive cultural activities, such as exploring Buenos Aires' iconic neighborhoods, attending tango music sessions, and enjoying street food and futbol matches.
 
The curriculum is designed to cater to different language proficiency levels. In the first week, participants explore their own identity or focus on Argentine cultural norms and history, depending on their language level. The second week involves learning to describe community places and discussing Buenos Aires' creative aspects, including music and street art. By the third week, participants engage in projects like creating travel flyers or public awareness campaign videos, further enhancing their language skills and cultural understanding. Highlights of the program include visits to cultural and historical sites, cooking classes, and weekend excursions to places like Iguazu Falls.
 
The program is led by experienced staff, including Carolina Zumaglini, the Center Director, who is an education consultant, historian, and professor from Buenos Aires. Program Leaders also guide and support students throughout their stay. There are no specific prerequisites mentioned, but participants are required to take a language proficiency test before starting the program to determine their language level. Upon completion, students will have improved their Spanish language skills and gained a deeper understanding of Argentinian culture, which can enhance college applications and future opportunities. College credit is available as an option for an additional fee.

title: Summer Arts Institute
description:
The Summer Arts Institute is a free, four-week program offered by NYC Public Schools, aimed at intermediate or advanced students in disciplines such as dance, theater, instrumental music, string orchestra, vocal music, film, and visual arts. The program's primary objective is to develop students’ artistic skills and creativity, broaden their awareness of the arts, and explore career options in arts and related industries. Participants have the opportunity to work with Department of Education arts specialists, cultural organizations, and guest artists, enhancing their portfolio or audition skills for future arts-focused education.

The curriculum offers a range of studio work and experiences, including rehearsals, attending professional performances, visiting arts venues, and career exploration. Students engage in hands-on projects and receive guidance from partnering organizations like the American Ballet Theatre, Carnegie Hall, and the New York Philharmonic. The program culminates in a final exhibition and performance, showcasing students' work.

The Summer Arts Institute is delivered in-person, providing a comprehensive arts education experience. To apply, students must complete an online application with a student essay and submit an online audition by the specified deadlines. This engaging program not only improves technical skills and creative expression but also connects students to community and cultural resources, promoting lifelong learning in the arts.

title: The Urban Leadership Fellows (ULF) Program
org: The Municipal Forum of New York, Inc.
description:
The Urban Leadership Fellows (ULF) Program is sponsored by The Municipal Forum of New York, Inc., and managed by Futures and Options. The purpose of the six-week, paid program is to enable New York City high school seniors to gain valuable hands-on work experience and training in the Municipal Finance industry.
In 1992, The Municipal Forum of New York, Inc. developed the Urban Leadership Fellows (ULF) Program with the mission of introducing talented young people to the municipal securities industry. The ULF Program is a paid, mentored summer internship program for graduating New York City high school seniors who are interested in careers in finance.
As ULF interns, eager young men and women experience the municipal securities sector first-hand and learn about various facets of the industry, from marketing, trading and underwriting to municipal credit research and analysis.
In 1996, The Municipal Forum engaged Futures and Options (F+O) to coordinate and manage the ULF Program with the goal of expanding internship opportunities and deepening its training component. Since its inception in 1995, F+O, has served more than 4,500 New York City youth and partnered with upwards of 500 businesses, nonprofit organizations and government agencies to offer high school students paid, mentored internships and career readiness training. The partnership between F+O and the Municipal Forum’s Youth Education Fund Committee has resulted in a steady rise in the number of firms sponsoring ULF interns. Currently, the Municipal Forum invites more than 40 students to participate in the ULF Program each summer.
Each student will be matched to a pod mentoring group with whom they will work throughout the program. MFNY Pod Mentors will meet virtually each week with students to assign tasks, guide work flow, provide feedback, and answer questions. MFNY Mentors will assign individual and group work-based projects.
To enrich their experience, ULF students will be also be paired with young professionals working in public finance who will serve as mentors to whom they can turn for professional advice as they enter careers in business and finance. Young Professional Mentors and students will meet for two events and communicate throughout the program. Young Professional Mentors will provide input to students on their assigned work work-based challenges and other assigned projects.

title: Food Culture in N.Y.C.
org: School of the New York Times
description:
New York City is home to an immense range of people and cultures. From food carts on many street corners, to markets providing a worldwide variety of foods to the highest-rated restaurants, New York’s food culture matches these identities. The culinary world includes different types of cuisines, chef roles and styles, food providers, markets and the business of restaurants. In addition, food authors, reviewers and critics play a key role in the success or failure of the food industry. This course will encourage students to deeply explore their own thoughts and ideas about food and food culture and to let their experiences inspire their own expressions via the lenses of contemporary food movements. In addition, students will meet the innovators, critics and observers of emerging trends in each of the fields that drive food conversation, and investigate how they are building their careers to succeed.

This multidisciplinary approach to learning encourages students to sample different fields and employ critical thinking and creative problem-solving skills. The course is geared toward students eager to discover their passions and try something new. From site visits and excursions to lectures by expert practitioners, the course is curated to give students a newfound sense of direction for their future studies by examining a diverse selection of subject material.

title: Introduction to Investigative Journalism
org: School of the New York Times
description:
Central to the mission of any topflight media outlet, investigative journalism is reporting on something that someone is trying to keep hidden. Police brutality and corruption, structural racism, school segregation, sexual harassment and political abuses of power are just a few examples of the recent issues covered by investigative journalists.

The best investigative journalism goes beyond day-to-day reporting to uncover systemic injustice and hold the powerful to account. Steeped in primary sources such as court documents and other public records, investigations usually take months – if not years – to hit the front page. When they do, their impact can be world- and life-changing for individuals and profound for specific communities. In this course, students will apply basic investigative principles such as understanding news value, finding and exploring primary sources documents, cultivating and protecting confidential sources, fact checking and working within the law.

title: Opinion Writing
org: School of the New York Times
description:
Students will develop and enhance their journalism skills, learn best practices for a changing professional environment and use New York City as the context for reporting and writing on a wide range of issues, from the personal to societal.

Throughout the course, students will work on developing strong research and critical thinking skills through instruction and practice in interviewing, researching and fact-checking. Students will also learn how to express their own voices on a wide range of current issues, from the personal to societal: how to be funny, surprise or go against the crowd. The course will explore how to harness facts to bolster a point of view and hone analytical prowess. In addition, masters of the craft will help students sharpen pitches as well as share tips to get published.

This course is taught by a wide variety of Faculty who will focus on their own areas of journalistic and topical expertise.

title: Photojournalism as Art
org: School of the New York Times
description:
Explore journalism through the art, technique and storytelling of photography. Students will develop a visual vocabulary through the study of important photographers and photojournalists as well as examine the intersection of art and journalism. As students expand their classroom out into the local neighborhood and the city, they will seek to translate what they see and feel into photographs that express an artistic and narrative perspective.

Outcomes for this course include a deeper understanding of what makes images visually, artistically and historically significant. Additionally, students can expect to gain technical photography literacy, participate in diverse field exercises and explore methods, ethics and opportunities for storytelling and journalism through this visual medium.

Technical know-how is not a class prerequisite; students may use either a smartphone or digital camera to complete the course. Faculty will accommodate students with a wide range of expertise, from those with a budding talent to those of more practiced levels of proficiency.

title: Sports Management
org: School of the New York Times
description:
This immersive program explores the dynamic and evolving world of sports business, offering students a comprehensive understanding of the industry’s unique structure and impact. Focused on professional and amateur sports, the program delves into key topics such as athlete management, facility and event operations, the interplay of culture and society with sports, and the future of the industry. Students investigate these themes through a combination of seminars, guest lectures, and site visits, gaining firsthand insights into the business of sports in New York City, a global hub for the sports industry.

Participants engage with industry professionals, including journalists, executives, and consultants, through lectures and networking opportunities. Previous guest speakers have included experts from MLB Network, NYCFC, CBS Sports, and Deloitte Consulting. Site visits have featured venues and organizations such as Yankee Stadium, Madison Square Garden, NFL Headquarters, and live sporting events, providing students with a behind-the-scenes look at the sports world.

The program culminates in a capstone project where students apply their knowledge to tackle real-world challenges, such as developing a business model for a new professional sports league. This hands-on learning approach equips participants with critical skills in analysis, teamwork, and strategic thinking.

Students can choose between residential and day program options, both offering access to all course activities. Ideal for rising 10th–12th graders and graduating seniors, this program is designed for students passionate about sports and eager to explore career opportunities beyond the playing field.

title: The Foreign Correspondent: Global Reporting
org: School of the New York Times
description:
As international journalism evolves in response to a rapidly changing media landscape, the role of the foreign correspondent is more complex now than it has ever been. In this course, students will investigate how this complexity affects correspondents who analyze events of international importance while simultaneously ensuring that people’s stories get told accurately and ethically.

Investigating ethics guiding the practice of journalism, students will also learn the mechanics of journalistic writing, challenge their personal assumptions and biases and explore the world with a curious eye. As a result, students will learn the role that journalists play in shaping reader understanding of the world and the policies that leaders set.

title: The New Narrative: Podcasting & Video Storytelling
org: School of the New York Times
description:
This course offers high school students an immersive introduction to the craft of narrative podcasting and video storytelling. Participants learn to develop a unique point of view and translate it into compelling audio and visual narratives. The curriculum is project-based, guiding students through the entire creative process from initial idea generation to a finished piece, with a focus on both documentary and fictional formats. Key skills covered include interviewing techniques, field recording, scriptwriting, and digital editing using industry-standard software.

The program is delivered online, combining synchronous workshops with independent project work. The instructional approach is highly collaborative, emphasizing hands-on creation and peer feedback. Students are expected to engage with both the technical and creative aspects of storytelling, developing their ability to structure a narrative and use sound and visuals effectively.

Instruction is provided by media professionals with significant experience in podcasting, filmmaking, and journalism. These instructors bring real-world expertise into the virtual classroom, offering practical insights and mentorship throughout the creative process. The program is designed for high school students with an interest in media and storytelling; no prior experience with audio or video production is required.

Upon completion, participants will have a polished piece for their portfolio and a foundational understanding of modern storytelling techniques. The program aims to build both technical proficiency and creative confidence, equipping students with skills in critical thinking, project development, and digital communication that are applicable to further academic pursuits and personal creative projects.

title: Hack on the Hill
description:
Hack on the Hill (HOTH XI) is a 12-hour beginner-friendly hackathon aimed at providing participants with their first hackathon experience. Hosted by ACM Hack, this event takes place in-person at Carnesale Commons Palisades Room on the Hill. The main objective is to foster creativity and build coding skills through hands-on projects, workshops, and mentorship. Participants will get the chance to work on exciting projects, receive technical help from mentors, and engage in fun social activities.

The hackathon includes various workshops and a Q&A session to help participants get started and refine their projects. Key topics covered during the event include intersectional environmentalism and interdisciplinary research, with workshops and panels led by UCLA professors and researchers. The event also features a meme competition and offers prizes for the best hacks in several categories, such as Best Overall, Best Web, and Best Beginner.

HOTH XI runs on a single day, starting with check-in at 8:00 AM and concluding with the closing ceremony at 10:30 PM. Participants can form teams of up to four people or choose to work individually. The hackathon is open to everyone, though UCLA students are given priority for prize tracks and in-person participation.
By the end of the event, participants will have enhanced their coding skills, gained experience in project development, and had the opportunity to network with peers and professionals. The hackathon aims to empower participants with the confidence and knowledge to tackle future coding challenges.

title: Ohlone Hacks: A Premier Hackathon for Bay Area Students
description:
Ohlone Hacks 2024 is a free, 12-hour hackathon held at Ohlone College in Fremont, CA, designed to foster innovation and creativity among participants. title: Penn Medicine Summer Program
description:
The Penn Medicine Summer Program provided by BOLD Summers offers high school students an immersive experience into the medical field, leveraging the resources of the University of Pennsylvania's Perelman School of Medicine. This in-person program spans four weeks and is structured to provide both theoretical and practical exposure to various medical specialties. Participants benefit from lectures and mentorship by esteemed Penn Medicine professors and doctors, contributing to a rich learning environment.

The curriculum covers a wide range of medical topics and skills. Students learn CPR techniques, first aid, emergency care, suturing, and surgical knot tying. They also acquire skills in identifying lung and heart sounds, performing skin biopsies, drawing blood, starting IVs, and conducting spinal taps. Additionally, the program includes labs in gross anatomy, pathology, microbiology, and urine analysis, and offers the chance to observe live or simulated surgeries. Over 20 doctors and clinicians from multiple specialties contribute to the program, ensuring a comprehensive educational experience.

The program's schedule includes morning and afternoon sessions filled with educational activities, while evenings and weekends offer social and recreational opportunities. Participants engage in diverse evening activities and weekend trips to regional destinations like New York City and Washington, DC, enhancing their social experience and cultural exposure. Admissions open in late November, with applications due by late February. Admission decisions are communicated by the end of March, and students must promptly confirm their participation with a tuition deposit. Current medical students assist in small group settings, providing personalized instruction to ensure the mastery of techniques.

The Penn Medicine Summer Program is open to high school students, offering them the chance to gain valuable insights into the medical profession, solidify their interest in pursuing a medical career, and make connections with peers and professionals in the field.

title: Project ENGAGES
description:
Project ENGAGES is a high school science education program developed by Georgia Tech, in partnership with seven minority-serving public high schools in the City of Atlanta. This program primarily serves African-American students, many of whom receive free or subsidized lunch. The program consists of three main components: Science Outreach Initiative, Research Experiences for Students, and Science Training for Teachers. 

The Science Outreach Initiative aims to provide in-depth exposure to science for students through direct interactions with Georgia Tech researchers and laboratories, high bandwidth video conferencing, and the establishment of science clubs. The Research Experience for Students component allows students to conduct their own research projects under the mentorship of an engineering or science researcher. These research experiences extend for a 12-month period, during which students work full-time in the summer and 15 hours per week during the school year. Prior to undertaking their own research, students participate in a 4-week summer "Boot Camp" to learn the necessary scientific research skills. The student's completed project will then be presented at a science fair competition.

The Science Training for Teachers component allows science teachers from partner schools to gain exposure to engineering and science research and acquire laboratory skills by training with a Georgia Tech research scientist. This equips teachers to expand their classroom curriculum into new areas of science and engineering, thereby having a wider impact on science education in the partnering high schools.

Project ENGAGES is co-directed by Professors Tequila A. L. Harris and Edward Botchwey, with the assistance of Lakeita Servance, Assistant Director of Outreach Initiatives, and Michael J. Glenn, Ed.D., Program and Operations Manager, Outreach K-20 Programs. The program is open to high school students with an interest in science, biology, bioengineering, biotechnology, physics, and other areas of engineering, such as environmental engineering, materials, and robotics. Interested students are expected to spend a significant amount of time in their Georgia Tech research laboratory and participate in enrichment programs.

title: Art, Community, Education and Science Internship
description:
The Art, Community, Education and Science (ACES) Internship at Wave Hill is a year-long paid program that encourages high school students to delve into the convergence of community engagement and science-informed, informal arts education. The internship is designed for students who have a strong interest in arts and science education and are comfortable interacting with the public, including children.

The participants are expected to work four to eight hours each weekend, assisting with various tasks required for the successful execution of the Family Art Project. These responsibilities may range from organizing and cleaning up to communicating educational information to families and assisting in creating sample art projects or educational displays. Moreover, they will also be involved in facilitating storytime sessions and related activities.

The curriculum of the program aims to develop skills in public speaking and informal STEAM education, provide an understanding of program development, and aid in the creation of a personal work portfolio. Interns have the opportunity to participate in workshops focused on STEAM education and college readiness, all of which are based on Wave Hill's exhibitions, programs, and natural areas.

The program follows a schedule that includes two mandatory Saturday sessions per month, from 2:00PM to 4:00PM, during which interns foster connections to community, science, and the arts with the support of Wave Hill's staff. Additionally, as part of their final project, the interns work collectively to develop a weekend workshop for Wave Hill families, thereby gaining hands-on experience in program design, art education, and making connections to science and community.

To be eligible for this internship, students must be enrolled in high school and at least 16 years old by a certain date of the year. They should be eligible to work in the United States and available to participate for the full 13 months, starting June. Regular weekend availability from 9:00AM–4:00PM is also a prerequisite.

Upon successful completion of the program, interns will be compensated at an hourly rate of $15 and will have developed significant skills in public speaking, informal STEAM education, and program development. They will also have a portfolio of personal work, and will have explored potential career paths through meetings with educators, professional artists, and other nonprofit staff.

title: Forest Project
description:
The Forest Project has engaged countless New York City youth in ecological restoration and urban ecology since its inception in 1981. This paid summer internship gives high school students an unparalleled opportunity to learn about ecology in an urban setting. Throughout the summer, interns gain hands-on field experience and participate in dynamic, field-based coursework.

The work is rigorous but satisfying—building and maintaining woodland trails, removing invasive plant species, shoring up eroded slopes, and helping to document the process of restoring the disturbed woodland toward a more balanced state. Team-building activities help create a strong sense of community, allowing interns to develop connections with each other and to the program's mission.

title: Kupu ‘Āina Corps (KAC)
description:
Kupu 'Āina Corps (KAC) is a year-long workforce development initiative established through the State of Hawai'i's legislation for a Green Jobs Youth Corps. The core objective of the program is to provide paid, career-building employment in the green jobs sector to individuals seeking alternative career paths. By helping Hawai'i's government, non-profit, and for-profit entities, KAC contributes to the expansion of the green economy.

Participants of the program can expect to gain significant experience in Hawai'i's environmental sector through Kupu's extensive network of partner organizations. The positions offered span across sustainability, agriculture, environmental technology, and other green job fields. The program is based in Hawai'i and is conducted in-person, offering both full-time and part-time roles for a duration of one year.

The KAC program bestows numerous benefits, including the opportunity to receive resume-building certifications and network with potential future employers. Participants are also paid between $18 to $20 per hour, depending on the placement, and are offered medical insurance if they work more than 20 hours. Additionally, retention bonuses and paid time off are granted.

The eligibility criteria for the program require applicants to be between the ages of 17 to 38 for the duration of the program term. They must be a U.S. Citizen, U.S. National, U.S. Permanent Resident, or otherwise legally permitted to work in the United States. Applicants must arrange their own personal housing and transportation on-island. Prior experience in the environmental sector is not mandatory, although it might be preferred for some positions.

Upon completion, participants will have improved their skills, added important credentials to their resumes, and made a significant contribution to the green economy. With the acquired skills and experience, they will be well-equipped for a career in the green job sector.

title: Nā Manu ʻElele
description:
Nā Manu ʻElele, also known as the Hawaiʻi Land Steward Program, is a collaborative initiative between Kupu and the State of Hawaiʻi’s Department of Land and Natural Resources (DLNR). The program's main objective is to provide temporary work and training opportunities for local “stewards” who assist in interpreting the historic nature, natural history, and safety concerns of sensitive natural areas. The program aims to mitigate unwanted behaviors and ensure safety and empowers local communities to manage and mitigate high visitor use in sensitive heritage areas.

The program will take place on the islands of Hawaiʻi, Maui, Oʻahu, and Kauaʻi, and a total of twenty-four stewards will be placed across these sites in part-time or full-time positions. The program usually starts in mid February 12, 2023, with an application deadline of December 15, 2023, however, applications will remain open until all positions are filled.

Interested applicants must be over the age of 17, be a US Citizen, US National, US Permanent Resident, or otherwise legally allowed to work in the United States. They must also be responsible for their own personal housing and transportation arrangements on-island.

Participants in the Nā Manu ʻElele Land Steward Program can expect to gain a deeper understanding of the historic nature and natural history of sensitive heritage areas in Hawaiʻi. They will also develop skills in managing visitor behavior and ensuring safety in these areas. Participants will receive a wage of $18/hr paid biweekly and may be eligible for health coverage if working 20+ hrs/week for 4 consecutive weeks.

title: NASA Summer Internships
description:
NASA's internship programs are designed to provide students with training, mentoring, and career development opportunities while working alongside esteemed professionals in science, engineering, financial, information technology, and business sectors. These programs are part of NASA’s Office of STEM Engagement (OSTEM) and offer paid opportunities for college-level students to contribute to NASA's mission of advancing science, technology, aeronautics, and space exploration.

The OSTEM internships allow students to gain practical work experience by working full-time or part-time at a NASA center or facility. Interns are paired with mentors who are research scientists, engineers, and professionals from various fields, enhancing their career readiness and building valuable technical skills. The program includes three sessions annually, with specific deadlines for summer and fall applications.

NASA also offers the Pathways program, which provides current students and recent graduates paid internships that serve as a direct pipeline to full-time employment at NASA. This program offers work-study (co-op) opportunities, paid federal civil servant positions with benefits, and professional development across most NASA facilities. Completion of the Pathways program may lead to a job offer at NASA. Interns typically work regular business hours, Monday through Friday, but schedules can vary based on project requirements.

While specific instructors and prerequisites are not mentioned, the internships include working with experienced NASA mentors on agency projects. Interns are responsible for their housing arrangements and may need a car depending on their center location and available public transportation options. Outcomes of the internship include gaining confidence, essential technical skills, and career readiness, preparing participants for successful entry into the workforce and potentially receiving a job offer from NASA.

Completion of the Pathways program may lead to a job offer at NASA. Interns typically work regular business hours, Monday through Friday, but schedules can vary based on project requirements.

While specific instructors and prerequisites are not mentioned, the internships include working with experienced NASA mentors on agency projects. Interns are responsible for their housing arrangements and may need a car depending on their center location and available public transportation options. Outcomes of the internship include gaining confidence, essential technical skills, and career readiness, preparing participants for successful entry into the workforce and potentially receiving a job offer from NASA.
trpcData.costInfo: Majority of interns receive a paid stipend award. Interns are responsible for their own housing and travel expenses.
trpcData.scheduleInfo: Interns typically work during the hours of 8 a.m. to 5 p.m., Monday to Friday.
trpcData.admissionInfo:
Components:
- No letter of recommendation is required.

Application Timeline:
- Applications for the summer session are typically due by late February.
- Applications for the fall session are typically due by late May.

How to Apply:
- Apply via the website's application form.

Other:
- Internship opportunities are offered in spring, summer, and fall sessions.
trpcData.eligibilityInfo:
Requirements:
- Must be a U.S. citizen.
- Must have a cumulative 3.0 GPA (on a 4.0 scale).
- Must be a full-time or part-time college-level student enrolled in a minimum of 6 semester hours.
- Must be a minimum of 16 years of age at the time of application.
- Undergraduate and graduate students must be enrolled in a degree-granting program at a U.S. Department of Education accredited college or university with a course of study aligned to NASA’s needs.
- Educators are also eligible to apply if enrolled as students.
- If a student has completed an undergraduate degree and is enrolled in graduate school the following semester, they are eligible for an internship during the gap period.

title: Research Apprenticeship Program (RAP)
org: North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University (N.C. A&T)
description:
The Research Apprenticeship Program (RAP) at North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University is a comprehensive, hands-on learning opportunity for high school students interested in food, agricultural, and environmental sciences. This two-week residential program is designed for rising juniors and seniors and takes place on the university's campus from June 15 to June 27, 2025. Participants engage in independent research projects under the guidance of experienced research scientists, providing a practical and immersive experience in scientific inquiry and research methodologies.
 
 The program emphasizes experiential learning, allowing students to conduct independent research and gain firsthand experience in scientific processes. Participants benefit from working alongside top-notch research scientists, enhancing their understanding of the scientific research landscape. The program is fully funded, except for a $25 non-refundable application fee, and includes a stipend for participants who successfully complete the program and enroll in one of the related majors at the university's College of Agriculture and Environmental Sciences.
 
 Eligibility for RAP includes U.S. citizenship, a minimum GPA of 2.8, and being a rising junior or senior in the fall of 2025. Students can only participate in the program once. This opportunity not only enhances students' research skills but also provides a pathway towards academic advancement in agricultural and environmental sciences.

title: Summer Youth Employment Program (SYEP)
org: NYC Department of Youth & Community Development
description:
The Summer Youth Employment Program (SYEP) is the nation’s largest youth employment program, connecting NYC youth between the ages of 14 and 24 with career exploration opportunities and paid work experience each summer.

Participants have the opportunity to explore their interests and career pathways, develop workplace skills and engage in learning experiences that help in developing their social, civic and leadership skills.

By participating in structured project and work-based opportunities, NYC youth are better prepared for careers of the future. 

Special programming available for NYC youth through various service options:
- NYC Teens aged 14—15 learn about career opportunities and make a difference in their communities through paid project-based activities
- Young people aged 16–24 improve their work readiness skills and explore career pathways through paid summer jobs in a variety of industries throughout NYC

What the Program Offers?
- Work Readiness
- Project-Based Learning
- Financial Literacy
- Summer Jobs

title: Theatre Arts Division
description: The NHSI Theatre Arts Division offers a powerful exploration into stage performance and design. A rigorous combination of classes and rehearsals challenge students every day for five weeks, giving them a taste for what their college experience may be like should they pursue a degree in the field. Working with professional theatre practitioners from around the country, students are exposed to a multitude of performance styles, receive extraordinary training in a collaborative and supportive environment, and get to take advantage of the world-class facilities offered at Northwestern. All students will perform in or stage manage a fully produced, 60 minute play directed by a professional director. Every student leaves with an expanded understanding of what is possible in Theatre.

1. Submit the National High School Institute Application including $50 application fee.

trpcData.eligibilityInfo:
1. The Theatre Arts Division of the NHSI is a competitive program. Application decisions, made by the Admissions Committee, are based on ability and merit. Enrollment is limited.
2. Students must be current high school juniors who will be rising into their senior year of high school following their summer at Northwestern
3. Should rank academically near the top 30% of your high school class
4. Meet a high standard of character, responsibility, and intelligence
5. Able to provide evidence of experience and/or substantial interest in the theatre arts

externalReviews.title: Theatre Arts Division
externalReviews.content: I learned ways to trust myself, stay in the moment, and get out of my head...The things I learned in this program will be with me for the rest of my life...The staff truly made it feel like you were working with professionals, which is what one looks to get out of a program as rigorous as this. At the same time, however, each and every one of them was approachable, willing to exchange advice, and personable in their own right…and that is something not every summer program can offer....A noncompetitive, safe and encouraging atmosphere to learn...My favorite part of the program was the freedom to explore my art without any limits or hindrances.
externalReviews.authorDescription: Official at Northwestern National High School Institute

externalReviews.title: RPA pair accepted into Northwestern summer theatre arts program
externalReviews.content: I'm beyond words excited to attend Cherubs this summer, Sorensen said. It's an incredible opportunity to work with professionals in a university setting, where I get to be pushed outside of my comfort zone, past my current limits, as well as have a ton of fun. I'm honored to be one of eight stage managers from across the nation and world to be chosen for Cherubs, Lawton said. I'm excited to have the opportunity to learn more about the traditional stage manager experience as well as diving into a new perspective of theatre, while working with professionals from all over.

externalReviews.title: Stage Management/Design-Tech Concentration
externalReviews.content: Backstage and design work is brought into the spotlight in this program. All of the Design/Technical classes will give students tremendous opportunities for individual instruction, with a teacher-student ratio of approximately 1:2. In this program students will get the opportunity to work one-on-one with some of the most innovative designers in Chicago today...."I learned so many things about lights that I can apply to work back home.”...The scenery shop was huge. However, I learned my way around and I feel like I got the best experience while working in a top-notch shop.”..“The most professional, high quality program I’ve ever done.”
externalReviews.authorDescription: Official at Northwestern National High School Institute

externalReviews.title: Theatre Arts Musical Theatre Extension
externalReviews.content: Students will receive classes in Voice, Dance, Acting for the Song, and more from experienced NHSI and Northwestern faculty. At the conclusion of the 2-week program, students will perform a song cycle based on the work explored in their classes..."I learned a lot about how to act the song and play a character I had never played before. I really enjoyed working on the [musical] production.”
externalReviews.authorDescription: Official at Northwestern National High School Institute

externalReviews.title: NHSI general idea?
externalReviews.content: The theater kids were all grouped together in a great dorm at Northwestern which had its own common areas and a cafeteria. I think the journalism kids shared the cafeteria but not the dorm. Hers was an amazing, life changing experience...She felt that she was surrounded with so many talented kids and that the instruction was incredibly high. They saw several shows -- both at Northwestern and in Chicago and had several trips into Chicago as a group. There were also Master Classes and a "College Night" where kids could question the faculty about different schools etc. The program did not specifically prepare you for the audition process, however.

externalReviews.title: Northwestern University Theatre Program "Cherubs"
externalReviews.content: Yes, the NHSI cherub program is a spectacular program that was no doubt life-altering for me and the many other who attended some years ago. Many of us went on to NYU, some to NU, and other all over the country. The classes there are incredibly intense and equally worth it. The productions put on in the end are top notch and the facilities are excellent. I couldn't give this program a higher recommendation. And lucky for you, i know for a fact that they offer some very hefty financial aide packages to those that apply. So I would encourage you greatly to go for it. You won't regret it.

title: Tulane Pre-College Programs
description:
Tulane Pre-College Programs offer high school students the opportunity to explore various academic disciplines while preparing for college life. The programs are designed to provide a supportive environment where students can develop academically and personally. Participants can choose from enrichment courses that focus on expanding academic horizons without the pressure of exams and grades, as well as credit-bearing courses that provide a more intensive learning experience similar to a Tulane first-year course. 

The curriculum covers a wide range of subjects, including humanities and STEM fields, allowing students to gain skills in creative problem-solving, communication, and research. Enrichment courses are weeklong, while credit-bearing courses last two weeks and offer three hours of college credit upon successful completion. These programs are delivered both in-person on Tulane's campus and through a residential experience, where students take two enrichment courses during their stay.

To participate, students must meet specific eligibility criteria: rising 9th through 12th graders for enrichment courses and rising juniors and seniors for credit-bearing courses. Residential participants must be at least 14 years old by the start of their session. The main benefits include exposure to college-level academic subjects, the opportunity to earn college credit, and the development of independence and confidence through immersive experiences in New Orleans. Successful completion of an enrichment course results in a certificate of completion.

title: Characterizing the Urban Land Surface Temperature via an Innovative, Multi-Platformed Suite of Satellite and Ground-Based Remote Sensing Technologies (GISS/CUNY)
description:
In light of climate change, urban micro-climates, the urban heat island effect and other urban geophysical phenomena and processes, there is a new urgency to better study, understand, and characterize urban environments. Revolutionary and innovative ideas are being considered to transform the study of the urban landscape. Fundamental changes are taking place in geophysics and in engineering to aid in the adaptation and mitigation of the environmental challenges to which cities must respond.

For this project, students will perform a local, intensive, and comprehensive surface energy balance data collection and processing initiative that will help to characterize the urban heat island, the heat index, and more particularly the land surface temperature over various local community built and natural environments. The project aims to produce high temporal and spatial resolution land surface temperatures for the local community and for New York City using the combination of satellite remote sensing observations and ground-based measurements. Students will obtain remote sensing data from multiple polar orbiting and geostationary satellites. Additionally, students will use infrared cameras and flux tower instruments to understand how urban surfaces react to solar radiation and its consequent heat. Students will be able to monitor the incoming and outgoing radiation and heat energy components using the cameras. The differences between traditional rooftop materials and new green or white roofs will be explored. Moreover, hand held temperature measuring devices, Unmanned Aerial Systems (UAS), and observations from satellite infrared observations will be collected. Using statistical approaches and data processing, the gaps in temporal and spatial coverage appropriate for the development of a heat index (effect of air temperature +
humidity) will be filled. The volume of data used in this project is expected to in the range on 5TB. The added-value of this initiative is that cross-pollination between students and the local community and the transfer of knowledge between the two groups will be created and sustained long after the project ends.

Project Activities Include:
• Monitoring thermal characteristics of urban surfaces such as concrete, asphalt, rooftop, and vegetated surfaces at different seasons and times of the day by collecting data
• Coordinating with community partners to receive skin temperature measurements from various surfaces in the local community.
• Obtaining and analyzing satellite land surface temperature observations from geostationary and polar orbit satellites such as from the Geostationary Operational Environmental Satellite-R Series (GOES-R), LandSat, Ecostress, Sentinel 2A, the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS), etc.
• Analyzing the collected data to define and to develop a high spatial resolution (10 m) and high temporal resolution (every 5 min) skin temperature over the local community and over New York City using several statistical approaches by fusing satellite based and ground observations.
• Developing an online interactive server platform to disseminate the data to the local community and to scientists. Data visualization and queries will be among important features of the proposed platform.
• Working closely with the local community on the use of the collected data to interpret and predict the strength and extent of heat wave events.

• Obtaining and analyzing satellite land surface temperature observations from geostationary and polar orbit satellites such as from the Geostationary Operational Environmental Satellite-R Series (GOES-R), LandSat, Ecostress, Sentinel 2A, the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS), etc.
• Analyzing the collected data to define and to develop a high spatial resolution (10 m) and high temporal resolution (every 5 min) skin temperature over the local community and over New York City using several statistical approaches by fusing satellite based and ground observations.
• Developing an online interactive server platform to disseminate the data to the local community and to scientists. Data visualization and queries will be among important features of the proposed platform.
• Working closely with the local community on the use of the collected data to interpret and predict the strength and extent of heat wave events.

title: Climate Change Research Initiative Internship - GISS
description:
The NASA Earth Science Division’s Climate Change Research Initiative (CCRI) offers high school students an immersive summer internship focused on climate change research. Participants join interdisciplinary research teams led by NASA scientists at locations such as the Goddard Institute for Space Studies, Lamont Doherty Earth Observatory, and CUNY City College of Technology in New York City, as well as the Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, MD. Over six weeks, interns work collaboratively on NASA research projects, gaining hands-on experience and contributing to studies on topics like urban heat, extreme climate events, and monitoring lakes and estuaries from space.

Interns engage in a variety of activities, including data analysis, research discussions, and the creation of scientific posters and publishable papers. These projects culminate in presentations at NASA Headquarters and professional conferences. Through this experiential learning model, students gain valuable insights into NASA’s research processes while developing essential skills in scientific communication and collaboration.

This opportunity is designed for students with strong interests in STEM fields such as climate science, hydrology, and atmospheric studies. Participants are mentored by NASA researchers and collaborate with undergraduate interns, educators, and graduate students. The program combines in-person and virtual elements, offering flexibility while maintaining rigorous academic and practical engagement. Upon completion, interns leave with a deeper understanding of climate science and a strong foundation for future academic and professional pursuits.

title: Deciphering Changing Probabilities of Extreme Climate Events in Climate Models and Measurements (GISS)
description:
Extremes in precipitation and temperature are the realization of climate change most
familiar to us in our daily lives. Will the "100-year storm" now arrive every other year? In
this project, CCRI participants will look at simulations from the GISS climate model and
constrain them against satellite retrievals in the present day. How many years does it
take for the probability of extreme weather to become noticeable? The students will then
look at simulations of the future and the past and apply the same analyses to see how
the incidence of 'extremes' changes through time.

title: Monitoring and Studying Lakes from Space in a Changing Climate (GISS/CUNY)
description:
Climate change has impacted all components of the environment, and the impacts on global lakes have been quite noticeable. There are over 100 million lakes on Earth (excluding those that are covered with glaciers), covering roughly 4% of the total land surface. Natural lakes and man-made reservoirs are essential sources of freshwater, and they provide inarguably important services to society. They are used for fresh drinking water, municipalities, recreational activities, and fisheries. Moreover, lakes play a major role in carbon sequestration and thereby are critically important for our planet. Many lakes have been desiccated by the adverse impacts of climate change, and their ensuing degraded water quality has led to major losses in economic and ecological value as they have now become significant societal and health risks. In addition to climate change desiccation, lakes are dying and degrading due to human mismanagement, point and non-point source pollution, and general loss. The extent and rate of global “lake-loss” is not fully understood. Therefore, many aspects of in-land water bodies require robust, comprehensive study and monitoring in order to achieve sustainable environments, habitats, economies, and agriculture. Spaceborne remote sensing observations with their unique spatial and temporal coverage have considerable capabilities for supporting investigations of the Earth system including inland water bodies. This project, therefore, focuses on the application of satellite remote sensing and geographic information system techniques complimented by ground observations to study lakes and to provide insights about “lake-health” and about “lakeresponse” to the adverse impacts of climate change. Interns will obtain and analyze satellite data from geostationary and polar orbiting satellites such as the Geostationary. Operational Environmental Satellite-R Series (GOES-R), LandSat, ECOSTRESS, Sentinel 2A, the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS), etc.

Successful applicants will work closely with their mentors in related lakes research
areas to:
• Analyze surface temperature and land cover change trends of major global lakes
using daily infrared-based satellite sensors;
• Perform validations of satellite-based products such as surface water
temperature estimates (among others) using ground observations;
• Develop regional algorithms to predict Chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) and Harmful Algal
Blooms (HAB) concentrations using high resolution satellites such as LandSat
and Sentinel 2A;
• Predict and study regional and global ice phenology in lakes and thereby define
the impacts of climate change on ice-in and ice-out timing.

Duty location also at CUNY New York City College of Technology, Downtown Brooklyn
