title: Science Internship Program (SIP)
description:
The Science Internship Program (SIP) is a selective, summer research internship that places motivated high school students into active research groups at the University of California, Santa Cruz. Interns join existing projects in science, engineering, social sciences, humanities, and art, working under the close mentorship of UCSC faculty, graduate students, and postdoctoral researchers. Rather than following pre-designed classroom labs, students contribute to authentic, open-ended investigations where methods, results, and next steps evolve over time.

The program typically spans about eight weeks and combines an initial online research preparation period with full-time, in-person work on campus. Interns spend most days embedded in their assigned lab or research group, meeting regularly with mentors to plan experiments or analyses, discuss primary literature, and review progress. Schedules are flexible and set collaboratively by mentors and interns to reflect the needs of the specific project.

Across projects, students gain experience in skills such as reading scientific journal articles or other primary sources, writing code for scientific computing, conducting laboratory or field work, managing and analyzing data, and communicating research findings in both written and oral forms. The program culminates in a symposium where interns present their work to peers, mentors, and families, mirroring the structure of professional academic conferences.

SIP emphasizes mentorship, critical thinking, and access. Interns learn how individual tasks connect to larger research questions, participate in team problem-solving, and observe the culture of a research community firsthand. The program actively recruits students from historically excluded backgrounds in STEAM and aims to provide a transformative experience that can shape students’ academic pathways and readiness for future research opportunities.

title: SnowEx and Understanding the Role of Snow and Measurements (GSFC)
description:
Snow is an important part of Earth’s climate system, helping to regulate Earth’s surface temperature, as well as providing a freshwater source. More than one-sixth of the world’s population (1.2 billion people) relies on seasonal snowpack and glaciers for, water supply, agriculture, hydropower, and recreation. Snow cools the planet and supports many ecosystems. Snow melt can contribute to widespread and damaging floods.

We need accurate snow information to respond to altering climate and water availability
and predict future snow resources.

There are four features that need to be measured:
1. The areal extent and location of the snow
2. How long the snow lingers
3. The depth and water equivalent of the snow
4. How the snow is changing

Snow varies in space, making it difficult to validate on the ground what is observed from above. Landscape and vegetation characteristics can mask or impact remote sensing capabilities. Snow also varies is time. Changing snow characteristics, such as depth, grain size, density, and liquid water content, influence the signals observing snow.

SnowEx is a multi-year field experiment, which includes extensive surface-based observations to evaluate how to best combine different remote sensing technologies to accurately observe snow throughout the season in various landscapes.

The team members will contribute to the development of the SnowEx meteorological data archive. This includes evaluating data collected during the 2017-2020 snow seasons for errors and assisting in formatting and analysis to prepare the data for archival. The candidates should have some experience with data analysis and scripting. Experience working in snow research is highly desired. The candidates will also help in preparing metadata, charts, and presentation material to introduce the data to the larger snow community.

title: Beyond Words
description:
The Scientific Study of Language to Understand our Communication, Culture and Self
“How Switching Languages Can Change Your Personality”
“Vocal Fry—It’s All About Power, Status and Gender”
“24 New Words Invented by Teenagers”
“Why Do People Have Slips of the Tongue?”

Headlines like these reinforce that language is all around us: It’s a common thread that ties human beings together in the face of our many differences. Whether spoken or signed, formal or informal—think of ever-evolving Gen Z slang or the Swiftie “fanilect” with its own vocabulary!—languages are everywhere. Linguistics, the scientific study of language, goes beyond the form of language—whether sounds or gestures—to the how of language. How do we as language users encode and decode our own messages without a lot of conscious thought? How is it that others may do it differently, whether they’re across the globe, or even just across the room? How do speech and language disorders happen, and what resources exist to provide support? Ultimately, when we recognize the diversity of human languages, we can more fully appreciate the diversity of the human experience and can leverage that enhanced sense of humanity for the good of the world’s future.

In this program, College of Science faculty will lead students in an exploration of linguistics. Students will investigate how language develops and functions as a personal, mental, and cultural phenomenon. Students will discover how the language we use does more than communicate particular messages: It also communicates something about us as individuals and as members of different groups, thus having enormous implications for how disparate groups understand and interact with each other.

title: MITES Semester
description:
The MITES Semester, provided by the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, is an immersive online pre-college program that focuses on science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM) subjects. It is a unique learning opportunity that allows high school juniors to expand their understanding of these disciplines, spanning from Machine Learning to Thermodynamics to Astrophysics. The program offers one project-based course and one supplemental core course in Calculus, Physics, Computer Science, or Science Writing and Communication, emphasizing the impact of STEM on society and equipping students to use their knowledge for societal betterment.

The MITES Semester is a hybrid learning experience, with classes delivered live and online during the evenings. This format accommodates students' daytime commitments and encourages them to work closely with their peers in the program. The program runs for six months, starting in the summer before senior year and extending through December. Typical summer schedule includes courses from Mondays to Thursdays between 5pm–10pm ET, with events and webinars on Sundays from 8pm–10pm ET. In the fall, mandatory online events occur weekly on Sundays from 8pm–9pm ET and on select evenings.

The program is led by a team of qualified instructors from MIT, who guide students through rigorous coursework and offer insights into the practical application of STEM concepts. The program also includes mentoring sessions with undergraduate mentors, helping students navigate the academic landscape effectively.

Eligibility for the MITES Semester is restricted to U.S. citizens or permanent residents who are high school juniors. Applicants are generally expected to have a passion for STEM, demonstrated through extracurricular activities, essays, and letters of recommendation. The program encourages students from underrepresented and underserved backgrounds to apply.

Upon completion of the MITES Semester, participants will have gained in-depth knowledge in their chosen STEM fields, developed their project-based learning skills, and gained a comprehensive understanding of the college application process. This includes academic, social, and practical life skills, tailored college application guidance, and a written evaluation from their instructor, which can be used as supplemental material for college applications.

trpcData.costInfo: Free. All program-related costs are covered by generous individuals, foundations, corporations, and MIT. Students only pay for transportation to and from MIT for the conference. Travel scholarships are available.
trpcData.scheduleInfo: This is a 6-month immersive online program. During the summer, it requires a time commitment of 25-30 hours per week, with all synchronous components happening between the hours of 5 and 10 pm ET. There are no classes on Fridays or Saturdays. During this time, classes are held from Monday–Thursday between 5pm–10pm ET, and events and webinars occur on Sundays from 8pm–10pm ET. During the fall, the program requires a time commitment of 3-5 hours per week, with all synchronous components happening between the hours of 8 and 10 pm ET, no more than 3 days per week. Required online events during the STEM Immersion Phase occur weekly on Sundays from 8pm–9pm ET and on select evenings throughout the fall.
trpcData.admissionInfo:
Components:
- Nomination by a teacher or counselor at the student's school
- Selection of preferred seminar during the nomination and application process

How to Apply:
- Apply through nomination by a teacher or counselor at the student's school.

Other:
- Students will be placed in one or more of their preferred classes if selected for admission.
trpcData.eligibilityInfo:
Requirements:
- Must be U.S. citizens or permanent residents.
- Must be high school juniors.
- Must be enrolled in 11th grade in any school setting, including public, private, and homeschool.

Preferences:
- The program strongly encourages students from underrepresented backgrounds in science and engineering, particularly those identifying as African American/Black, Hispanic/Latinx, Native American or Alaska Native, and Native Hawaiian or other Pacific Islander.
- Students from low socioeconomic means, which may be indicated by qualification for free/reduced lunch, are particularly encouraged to apply.
- Preference is given to potentially the first family member to attend college.
- The program encourages applications from students from families with an absence of science and engineering degrees.
- Students from high schools with low admittance rates to top-tier colleges, especially rural or predominantly minority high schools, are encouraged to apply.
